metadata.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

So because the structuring is tied to the presentation, that means it's really controlled by the sender. So the sender makes decisions like how to format the quoted text, how to localize the attribution line or the quoted headers. In the attribution line, usually they'll format the name and or addresses and that may not match what you see in the client because email clients may pull names out of the contacts app or something from the device. It's also a problem for inline replies, for long threads with a lot of inline comments, especially things that are kind of deeply nested. It's easy to lose track of who's said what's actually being replied to. And it's expensive in error prone for mail clients to try to determine these quoted sections, which you may want to do to collapse them for presentation or to remove them before other processing that you may want to do. So a couple examples of some of the problems. On the left, we can see that there's a conversation between three people. One of whom is using outlook with the client set to Chinese.
